title: Soft amaretti
subtitle: Chewy Italian almond macaroons
excerpt: Twenty-four soft amaretti: Italian almond biscuits made from ground almonds and egg whites, with a crackled surface and a chewy, sticky centre. Gluten-free. They take 15 minutes to prepare and 15 to bake.
sourceNotes: Soft amaretti (amaretti morbidi) are documented in Italian confectionery and baking writing from at least the eighteenth century. Recipes appear in pre-1928 Italian household cookery texts and in Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, public domain). The Saronno-style version uses apricot kernel paste; this domestic version uses ground almonds and a small amount of almond extract for the bitter note. Cross-referenced with Ligurian and Lombardian baking literature of the early twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin. They stay soft for 4 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 175°C. They should be crackled on top and just set. Do not overbake or they lose the chewy centre.
baking.decoratingTechnique: roll in icing sugar before baking

glossaryTerms.definition: Soft amaretti have a crackled, sugary surface and a chewy, almost sticky centre. The texture comes from the balance between ground almonds, egg white, and sugar: the egg white binds and the sugar draws moisture back into the biscuit as it cools. Rolling the balls in icing sugar before baking sets the crackled exterior. If the biscuits are overbaked, they dry out and lose the characteristic chew; they should be pulled from the oven while the centre still feels soft to a gentle press.

content.text: Soft amaretti are Italian almond biscuits with no flour: just ground almonds, egg whites, sugar, and a small amount of almond extract. The
is chewy in the centre, with a crackled icing sugar crust. Gluten-free. Twenty-four biscuits, ready in 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 165°C fan (175°C conventional). Line two baking trays with paper. Mix the ground almonds and caster sugar together in a large bowl. Add the egg whites and almond extract and stir until a sticky paste forms. It will be quite wet; this is normal. Roll tablespoon-sized portions into balls in your hands. They will stick: wet your hands lightly if needed. Roll each ball in the icing sugar until well coated, then place on the lined trays, spaced 4 cm apart.

Bake for 14 to 16 minutes, until the tops are crackled and the biscuits are just set when pressed lightly; the centre should still feel soft. Do not overbake. Leave on the trays for 10 minutes before transferring to a rack; they firm up as they cool.
